document:
Kilo represents the editable file as an array of `erow` structures stored in `E.row`. Each row has a zero-based `idx`, the original character buffer in `chars`, its byte length in `size`, a display-oriented `render` buffer, the rendered length in `rsize`, syntax highlight bytes in `hl`, and `hl_oc` to remember whether syntax highlighting saw an open multiline comment at the end of the row.

The rendered row exists because screen columns are not always the same as file characters. `editorUpdateRow` frees any old `render` buffer, counts tab characters, allocates enough space to expand them, and writes a new rendered string. A tab becomes spaces until the next 8-column tab stop. After the rendered string is rebuilt, `editorUpdateRow` calls `editorUpdateSyntax` so highlight metadata stays aligned with what will be displayed.

Rows are inserted with `editorInsertRow`. It refuses positions beyond the end, grows the row array with `realloc`, shifts later rows with `memmove` when inserting in the middle, increments shifted row indices, copies the provided text into a new `chars` buffer, initializes render and highlight fields, calls `editorUpdateRow`, increments `E.numrows`, and marks the file dirty.

Deleting a row is handled by `editorDelRow`. It does nothing if the requested row is outside the file, frees the row's heap allocations with `editorFreeRow`, shifts later rows upward with `memmove`, fixes their indices, decrements `E.numrows`, and marks the file dirty.

Character insertion is split between row-level and editor-level helpers. `editorRowInsertChar` inserts into one row, padding with spaces if the requested column is beyond the current end of the line. Otherwise it grows the line, shifts existing bytes right, writes the new character, updates the rendered row, and marks the file dirty. `editorInsertChar` maps the visible cursor and scroll offsets into file row and file column positions, creates empty rows as needed, calls `editorRowInsertChar`, then advances the cursor or horizontal scroll.

Newline insertion has special cases. `editorInsertNewline` computes the file row and column from the cursor plus scroll offsets. If the current row does not exist and the cursor is exactly at the logical end of the file, it inserts an empty row. If the cursor is at column zero, it inserts an empty row before the current one. Otherwise it splits the current row at the cursor, inserts the right-hand text as a new row, truncates the original row, updates its render data, moves the cursor to the next line, and resets horizontal position.

Deletion joins lines when needed. `editorDelChar` returns immediately at the start of the file or when there is no row. If the cursor is at column zero, it appends the current row's text to the previous row with `editorRowAppendString`, deletes the current row, moves the cursor up, and adjusts horizontal scrolling. Otherwise it deletes the character before the cursor with `editorRowDelChar` and moves the cursor left.

Opening a file is deliberately simple. `editorOpen` clears the dirty flag, replaces `E.filename`, and attempts to open the path for reading. A missing file is not fatal: if `errno` is `ENOENT`, it returns `1` and leaves the buffer empty so the filename can be saved later. For an existing file, it reads with `getline`, strips a trailing newline or carriage return, inserts each line as an `erow`, frees the temporary line buffer, closes the file, and clears the dirty flag again.

Saving converts the rows back into one heap buffer. `editorRowsToString` counts every row's size plus one newline, allocates that total plus a null terminator, copies row contents, appends a newline after each row, and returns the buffer length through an output pointer. `editorSave` opens or creates `E.filename` with mode `0644`, truncates it to the exact new length with `ftruncate`, writes the entire buffer in one call, closes the file, frees the buffer, clears `E.dirty`, and reports the number of bytes written. On an I/O error it closes any opened file descriptor, frees the buffer, leaves the dirty flag set, and reports the error in the status message.
